focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The rookery had gone past beautiful and into something sticky, loud, and faintly indecent. The spoonbills did not so much occupy the mangroves as overtake them. Pink bodies flashed from branch to branch. Bills clattered like someone shaking a crate of wet bones. Every exposed limb of mangrove seemed to hold a nest in progress, a nest collapsing, or a pair of birds trying to fuck on top of materials they had not finished stealing yet.

From the observation platform Mina could smell the colony before she could sort any individual bird from the mass. Late afternoon had cooked everything down to guano, sun-baked fish, green mangrove sap torn fresh from twigs, the sugary donor wine sweating in its metal tub behind her. The wind kept changing its mind and bringing all of it at once. Guests had paid extra for spoonbill week and the birds were delivering exactly the sort of spectacle rich people liked to pretend they had discovered with their own moral seriousness. Every few minutes somebody on the platform made a delighted little noise that was really embarrassment wearing a visor.

One male spoonbill lurched onto a nest already occupied by a female and three stolen sticks, flapped pink and white over her head, lost his footing, recovered it with a furious clack of the bill, then mounted her anyway while she stayed braced and practical beneath him. Two donors laughed, then looked around to see whether laughing at bird sex counted as vulgar. Another bird came in crosswise with a mangrove twig longer than its own body and got into a shoving match with the pair before the male finished. A shower of leaves and droppings came down through the branches. Somebody behind Mina said, "Oh my God," with the round, pleased horror of a woman seeing a bartender crack an egg into...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...the common-room couch, and went quiet again. The sound should have embarrassed Mina. Instead it folded the whole night back into scale: wet roof, lousy fish, engine diagrams on the table, a kid trying to sleep through a storm because trade school still cost money in the morning. Not a fantasy chamber. A camp held together by towels, batteries, and people taking turns being useful.

Rhea touched the old prop scar on Mina's calf with the side of one foot under the sheet. "Cold?"

"A little."

Tomas reached behind him for the thin blanket and dragged it over all three of them. "Better."

No one said what the state of things had become. Mina was grateful for that. Naming could wait until morning, when language would start making claims and asking for history. Right now the dangerous part was simpler. Tomas knew where to put the water glass so it would not tip if the house shook. Rhea tucked the blanket under Mina's shoulder as if she had already learned the draft pattern there. Mina's bag sat by their dresser instead of in her flooded room. The water glass on the crate, the blanket at her shoulder, the extra bag by the dresser: every object around the bed had shifted an inch to take her in, as if the space had been waiting on a name.

Outside, the storm kept worrying at the camp, testing every seam. Inside, the lantern burned lower. Rhea's thumb moved once against Mina's waist, a small absent stroke. Tomas's breath slowed at the back of her neck. Mina lay awake between them and understood with sick clarity that secrecy had been easier because it asked so little. A hidden thing could stay cheap. Shelter would cost more.

Water ticked somewhere down the hall into a bucket Beto had shoved squarely under the leak. The roof held. The walls held. For that one night, so did they.
